sentences.text: I wanted to mention that Polish prisoners of war in '39 were given the opportunity to leave the camp and to work as a civilian workers in Germany.
They would be paid wages, given the ration cards.
And they could live in a private rented apartments, houses, or-- or flats, rooms, whatever.
A lot of-- a lot of Polish prisoners of war took the option.
I don't if this was '41, around '41.
But when I arrived in '42, I met a lot of them wearing the P sign, for Pole, and working in different places.
One was a watchmaker, my friend, and one was working in a factory.
One was working-- some of them are working on farms.
But still, they were working, living privately, renting rooms, and earning some-- some money, wages.
And they had the ration cards, which were most important.
Because you couldn't buy anything in Germany without the ration cards, food ration cards and tax [? style ?] ration cards.
For-- so they-- a lot of Polish prisoners of war were-- took the option, and they were living like this in  Germany.
I arrived in Germany on the 24th of January, '42.
So just 10 days after leaving [INAUDIBLE].
Well, what I did, in Warsaw, I wrote a letter and another letter.
And I got a message after the war from one of my cousins who survived that they got my-- the message.
But to get into the ghetto, it was impossible.
A train was passing the ghetto from one side of Warsaw to the other side, just through the [INAUDIBLE] place.
And I decided to take train-- train and see if I can jump or I can get into whatever.
It was impossible, because the doors were locked.
And guards were everywhere.
But they couldn't change the road for some-- some technical reasons.
So they had to put the train through the ghetto and to the other part Warsaw for some technical reason, which I don't know.
I couldn't get in.
If I get in, I would never get out.
And I couldn't help-- I couldn't bring some help.
I could bring only misery and other-- and other mouths to feed and another son to-- to take care of.
It was no point of going and-- and sharing the same-- well, we didn't know what at this time.
At  this time, we knew that they are not in a good position.
But I knew that my father had money stocked away and some jewelry.
And I knew that he can survive, the family can survive.
I didn't suspect nothing.
Nobody knew anything.
Nobody could imagine what will happen.
No one could imagine.
That's why was no urgency.
I got-- I got the message through to my parents that I am going away for a good position in Germany.
And I didn't want to know what in-- didn't know-- didn't want to tell them exactly the place, because of the security.
And I was satisfied that they know that I am safe, that I can survive and nothing else.
I wrote letters later on, because I had the address.
And I think they got the letters.
But I never got an answer.
Well, here I am coming to Germany by train.
First thing, the-- I have something very important to say about Warsaw.
In Warsaw, when I got the papers to go to Germany, they send me to a center, where-- the center which was the place where they gathered all the people.
And they organized the transport.
Before I went to this place, I-- I was sent to the Gestapo center in [INAUDIBLE] street in Warsaw with other Polish people, men and women, for a medical examination.
And the medical examination, they took all the clothes for-- for cleaning, for dry cleaning, or disinfection.
And we were standing on the tables naked.
And the doctors were examining us, see if we have no diseases.
No, no-- they didn't want to bring diseases to Germany.
And the doctors were Polish doctors.
And I passed through this examination.
When I took my clothes back and sat down to have some sandwiches and [INAUDIBLE], I said, this is a miracle.
And this miracle happened.
I passed through.
And then I-- then our small group with an SS man, an old-- old man probably 50, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he took us into a Warsaw Berlin express train, departments, warm, cozy, nice.
Everybody was fed.
And we went from Warsaw to Berlin by train [? accident ?] to visit with the German officers and military and everybody, no problems.
When I arrived in Germany, in Berlin, I lost my [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] laces.
[INAUDIBLE].
My laces were torn.
And I had to buy laces.
And I didn't know how to say laces in German.
So I went to a bookshop and asked them if they have vocabularies.
They have a vocabulary.
This is only German-Polish.
Well, this is the one I want.
And I can show it this.
I have got it here both in Berlin in January '42.
So I bought the laces.
I had some food.

And the German officer left me with my papers and told me that from [NON-ENGLISH], which means the railway station, [INAUDIBLE] railway station, I will go to Brandenburg and so forth, so on, and to get to my town where I have my position.
So I went by myself from Berlin to Brandenburg.
Nobody was watching me.
I came to Friedland in Mecklenburg late in the afternoon, I remember, Friedland in Mecklenburg.
And the Polish man was waiting for me.
And they welcomed me.
They were very happy, because he's going home to his family.
And I got his room.
His room was very nice furnished with a beautiful bed, washing basin, no running water, toilet outside.
And the family was [?
Shultz ?] family, [?
Shultz, ?] of course, old-- older people whose son was in the army in Africa core, in Africa, [INAUDIBLE].
And the daughter was married with children someone living else.
So I got the room.
And I stayed in this room for three years.
I gave my name in the Warsaw [INAUDIBLE]-- Warsaw employment office as [?
Watslaw ?] [?
Friedaman. ?] Because I was afraid that they will check my Russian passport.
And in the Russian passport, I could only change from Jew to Polish and from Friedman to [?
Freidaman. ?] I couldn't change more, because it was too much.
And nobody saw it.
And nobody would see and nobody would believe it.
Because it was very bad change.
So my-- I came to Germany as [?
Watslaw ?] [?
Freidaman. ?] And this Polish man took me to the factory.
This was a potato produce factory.
They produce from potatoes different things like flours, and chips, and syrups, and other things only from the potatoes.
And the potato crop was huge in this area.
This is an area of potato growing.
The factory was working 10 months a year, nine months a year, not more.
Because the crop didn't come.
So this chap, I don't even remember his name, because I met him only a day or two and then he left, introduced me to the director of the factory, Herr Berman.
Herr Berman was a very distinguished gentleman, older man.
And he took me around to show me around and asked me my name.
[INAUDIBLE] Jewish?

This is the first thing that I was asked in Germany, if I am Jewish, by the director Berman So I asked him Herr Director, how could I be Jewish and sent by a Warsaw employment office to Germany to work?
It's impossible.
Ah, this is true.
And this was the end of it.
Three years later, they were fighting with the employment office in town to keep me.
Because I wanted to go somewhere else.
And I went somewhere else.
But this is another story.
Well, where are we?
I arrived.
I took over my room.
I went to the factory.
And my boss was-- in the factory, I had a laboratory, large room, just a room.
And the boss was Dr.
Winkler, ?] [?
Wolwram ?] [?
Winkler. ?] He was a German, distinguished German, gray hair.
He was a member of the Nazi party.
And when I came, he said, Heil, Hitler.
I said good morning.
This was the-- I would-- I wouldn't be supposed to say Heil, Hitler.
They didn't want us, the foreign [INAUDIBLE], to say Heil, Hitler, only good morning.
And he showed me around.
And he showed me around.
He taught me how to do it, what to do.

And then I was starting, you know, in the-- taking samples in the factory from diffident stages of production, brought to laboratory, checked it for sugar, for starch, and contents for water, and things like that, whatever.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the afternoon, 6 o'clock, I went home.
And I had everything-- all the meals prepared by the [?
Shultzes ?] family.
I paid them an amount which was half of my wages.
This was approximately the amount for everything, lodging and full board.
And the rest of the money, I could only play cards or nothing.
You couldn't buy anything.
But in the meantime, in the factory, I developed a very good relationship with a few Poles, which are working, and with the French.
There was ?] quite a group of French prisoners of war.
The prisoner-- the French prisoners of war were living in a camp.
And they were coming to the factory without any guards.
No point of running away, because there was no where to run away without clothing, without language, without nothing.
The-- the best thing was to live in the camp, because camp was quite comfortable.
The Red Cross parcels start coming end of '42, I think.
The post was working.
The communication with family was by letters.
So it wasn't so bad to-- to wait another year or two and to be released instead of running away and who knows where.
So the French prisoners of war, I had a very good relationship with [?
Robert ?] and other prisoners.
We had pictures together somewhere.
And then we started some commerce.
They were getting everything except fresh bread.
Because fresh bread, they needed everyday.
Biscuits and things were not-- not good enough.
So I had some-- I could organize fresh bread on-- on the ration cards.
It's not important how I did it, because I paid something more, so I could get the fresh bread.
And I supplied them  bread.
They supplied me with coffee, the two ounces Nescafe.
And this Nescafe was more than gold for the Germans.
And my German boss, Mr.
Winkler, ?] which was very, very polite, very polite to me.
And when he heard about the Nescafe, anything.
So I supplied him with Nescafe.
And he supplied with eggs or something else.
Usually, this was this fresh bread to the French, Nescafe, cigarettes.
Then I supplied cigarettes to the Poles and to others, because I didn't smoke.
So the moderation of cigarettes was-- I supplied to somebody who was working at butchers.
You can imagine what pieces of meat and fat and things, whatever.
So this was the life, the-- the commercial life in this town.
From time to time, the French had-- had a free day theater and dancing, whatever you call it, no girls.
And then they-- they produced a show, comedy, music, dancing.
And they brought prisoners of war from all over the place, Frenchmen, of course, because it was in French.
And I was the only one invited.
I got French uniform, French boots, and the cappy.
And I came to the camp.
Nobody from the guards knew that I am not a Frenchman.
Because they were coming and going.
And we spend two or three times.
We had such a [INAUDIBLE].
It was magnificent.
The French were-- some of them were highly professional musicians, or actors, or dancers, professional.
But no girls.
But they wear clothes like girls, just to have the partners in dancing.
So this was the bright side of the life.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I supplied the information to the Frenchman, whatever I could get from newspapers, or from radio, or from the BBC.
We were listening to the BBC, but not very often at the beginning.
At the later stage, very often.
And almost-- and one day, we were listening to the BBC with the retreating German soldiers together.
But this is stages, you know.
The life of the Poles was rather good.
Because they had-- some of the Poles have rented few flats in an apartment house, three or four flats.
And from-- in each flat they had two or three people.
So the concentration was that nine or 10 people were living almost in the same place.
So we were meeting-- this was-- this place was called-- this was a hostel, kind of hostel, called [?
Wintergarten. ?] I don't have the translation, [?
Wintergarten. ?] And this [?
Wintergarten, ?] we met in the evenings, Sundays, holidays.
And I have got pictures from the [?
Wintergarten. ?] Some of them played accordion.
Some of them played-- the Poles played violin.
One was playing flute.
And I think this is the lot.
But this was a lot-- a lot of music.
Now, we needed the girls to dance and to entertain.
Who was the provider?
I was the provider.
Because I met the Russian girls who came to a linen factory.
This was already in '43.
They brought some 50 or 60 girls, Russian girls, to work in a linen factory.
What is a linen factory?
They-- they-- they were working on flax and linen.
Whatever the-- I think the end product was some textiles.
And the girls were free to leave, to-- to go on Sunday.
So we invited them.
I spoke Russian.
And I spoke Polish and German and French, of course.
And the Frenchmen were very happy to meet the girls and to exchange chocolate and to invite them somewhere to-- to our places.
So the girls came to [?
Wintergarten. ?] And we had really [INAUDIBLE] dancing and music, and singing, and especially on such an occasion like the capitulation of Italy in '43.
It was Sunday, beautiful day.
The capitulation was in this-- and we opened the windows.
And we started music and singing.
And the Germans were passing, why the Poles are so happy?
But they knew why they are happy.
So this was approximately the life.
How much in support of the Nazis were the [?
Shultzes, ?] the family?
Absolutely not.
I took the picture of Hitler that was hanging in my room and gave [? to Mrs.
Shultz. ?] I told her to get rid of it.
And she was afraid.
She was, Mister, please, don't do it.
Oh, I will be in trouble.
She put it back.
And Mr.
Shultz, ?] who was a soldier in the First World War, he was afraid to open his mouth.
But I met people, Germans, who opened their mouths.
The chap who was the radio technician who was on the second floor when I was leaving, on Sundays, he used to open the BBC full and listening to the communiques.
I was running away.
I couldn't stand it all.
I was afraid to be at home.
Then I met people, Germans, who one German-- older again, from the First World War-- who used to come and bring me samples to the laboratory from the fields.
The water, which are flowing from the factory into the fields, had a percentage of starch.
And we wanted to reduce the percentage of starch.
Not to get rid of starch, we wanted to get rid of water.
So he was bringing from different places in the fields.
He was wearing the long boots.
And here, I have samples for you and give me the results.
In the meantime, he told me some stories about the Nazis.
And I-- he was so against the regime.
Oh, he was probably a Communist before the war.
There were a lot of Communists.
And he was so against.
He was not afraid to talk to me.
I was afraid to talk to him.
And then it was Mrs.
Foss. ?] [?
Mrs.
Foss ?] was the cleaning lady in our factory.
But her son was working on the syrups, on the boiling of the huge vats in syrup where I was taking samples.
And they were anti-Nazis, both of them.
But she was afraid to open her mouth.
And you could figure out what's happening if you knew that once they were fighting near [INAUDIBLE], which is near Leningrad.
And once, they  are already in East Prussia.
So you knew what's happened.
In general, you knew everything.
You knew about Stalingrad.
You knew everything.
My-- my-- my boss, told me about the Stalingrad.
Because I-- I was-- I was-- I wasn't talking to him about things-- news and things like this.
So he told me the news about Stalingrad, which I knew, and told me, you know, 100,000 German soldiers are [INAUDIBLE].
So I told him, you know what I heard about?
Quarter of a million.
But I think this is the only time I-- I-- I couldn't resist to tell him about something.
But in general, we didn't speak about this.
Go ahead.
What was your reaction to the defeat in Stalingrad of the Germans?
Well, actually we were celebrating.
Everybody was celebrating.
We were celebrating so that they won't see it.
With the Italian capitulation, we-- this was a [INAUDIBLE] opening.
But with the Stalingrad, first thing, it was winter.
And then we didn't-- it was such a tragedy for the Germans, that we simply didn't want to-- to elaborate more.
And they lost a lot of fathers and sons.
And it was a loss.
It was a huge loss.
I read two newspapers, the [INAUDIBLE], and [INAUDIBLE].
[LAUGHS] You laugh.
I got them subscribed.
The [INAUDIBLE], it is a newspaper of the [?
NSARP. ?] You know what is NS-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]-- in short, Nazi.
And the [INAUDIBLE] was a Berliner paper.
And these two were coming to my place.
And I was reading them often.
Oh, they wouldn't say nothing, absolutely nothing.
We were not aware.
Until the uprising in-- in the Warsaw ghetto, we didn't know, I didn't know nothing.
I knew this [INAUDIBLE] is not good, have to survive.
But I didn't nothing.
From the uprising in the ghetto, the Poles were talking between them.
Ah, I [INAUDIBLE].
In this group of Poles, as you saw on the picture, we were dancing.
We were enjoying ourselves.
We had music.
But once it came the topic of Jews, I wasn't there.
Not good and not bad.
Absolutely, this was my policy, you don't open your mouth.
Nothing.
You don't exist.
I don't know if this was good or not.
But still, this was my policy.
This was April '43.
The uprising of ghetto was Pesach, '43.
Well, to comment about it, what I could say good or bad, It was no point.
No point of commenting.
I didn't want to be involved at all.
To comment, it won't help anybody.
And it could only damage my position.
To say something bad, I wouldn't dare.
I wouldn't dare to say that some Poles said good to the Jews, you know, to be-- to be-- to show that I am more pious than the Pope, to tell the Jews, oh, good.
This, I would never do it.
And so I decided not to comment at all.
If the Poles talked between them, they talked between them.
I don't exist.
The reaction of the Poles was mixed.
I would say some of them were happy.
Good, the Jews are getting some.
Or some of them are not happy.
So it was not-- not exacly-- and then I didn't know, because I didn't discuss with them.
But I had two friends who were living in this [?
Wintergarten, ?] older than myself.
One was married and with two children.
And he was a prisoner of war.
He went eventually to see his family once.
He got permission.
And the other one was a watch maker, very nice fellow, already in his 30s.
So he wasn't such a youngster like us.
I think they knew exactly what I am.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I think now or later, I thought, it's simply impossible.
I came from a college with 27,000 Jews.
And he came from Plaszow.
There is no Jews at all.
And here is somebody who is called [?
Watslaw ?] [?
Friedaman. ?] He speaks a handful of languages, because you couldn't hide that you speak French with the Frenchman.
And then German, I must speak, because I am the-- working such a position.
And Polish, they knew I speak Polish.
And the Russian girls on top of it.
They didn't know that I speak Hebrew.
